facts_disclosed: Mother visiting from Toronto — March 28 weekend, 2025

turns.content: I need to vent about something before I lose my mind cleaning this apartment at midnight on a Monday
Go ahead — what's driving the midnight cleaning frenzy?

My mother is coming this weekend. Friday. March 28. And I love her, I really do, but the woman is going to walk through my door and within fifteen minutes she'll have reorganized my spice shelf and told me I look tired
The classic parent visit — love wrapped in unsolicited assessments of your lifestyle. Where is she coming from?

Toronto. She moved there about two years ago. Found a Russian community she likes, got a job at a bakery, she's happy there I think. But she worries about me being here alone and every visit is like a wellness inspection
That tracks — she's far enough away that she can't check in casually, so when she does visit it all comes out at once. How often do you see her?

Maybe every two or three months? She takes the train sometimes. It's not far but our schedules are opposite — she's up at 4am for the bakery, I'm getting home at midnight from service. When she visits she stays up waiting for me and then she's exhausted and it's this whole cycle of us both being tired and irritable
That's a tough rhythm. Two people who love each other but are physically running on completely different clocks. Is she staying with you for the whole weekend?

Friday to Sunday. And look, she's not wrong about any of it — I DO look tired, my apartment IS a mess, I probably DON'T eat enough at home. But hearing it from your mother when you're 33 years old is... blyat, it's a lot. She means well. She always means well. It's just that her love comes with commentary
There's something uniquely frustrating about someone being right and caring and still making you want to scream. Are you working this weekend too, or did you manage to get time off while she's here?

I got Saturday off which is basically a miracle. But Friday and Sunday I work. So Friday she arrives and I leave for service, Saturday we have the whole day together, Sunday morning she leaves and I go back to the kitchen. At least Saturday I can cook for her — that's when we're best, when there's food between us

Cooking together might be the best version of the visit — shared activity, shared language, and she can see you in your element instead of just seeing the tired version of you after a shift. Are you planning to make anything specific?

I was thinking about doing a proper Russian meal. Borscht, maybe some blini, something that reminds her of home. She'd never say it but I think she misses the food. Toronto has Russian places but it's not the same as someone cooking for you. When Babushka was alive she always cooked for Mama, and now nobody does that for her

That's a really thoughtful gesture — picking up the thread of care that your grandmother used to carry. She'll feel that even if she doesn't say it. And it gives you a role in the visit that isn't "daughter being inspected" — it's you taking care of her.

Okay now you're going to make me cry and I still have to scrub the bathroom. But yes. That's exactly it. Alright — apartment cleaning, then sleep, then four more days of anxiety before she arrives. Spasibo for listening
You'll survive the inspection. And the borscht will be worth every minute of the visit. Good luck with the bathroom, and enjoy Saturday with her.
